purpose: Draft (inactive) Chapter 5 question objects derived from the Water Survival Student Manual excerpt (pages 120-132) processed in vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted. Category assignments (ready for review / needs verification / unclear-OCR) live in vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-promotion-review.md, not in this file.
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified' as part of an explicitly-requested promotion task.
sourceHandlingNotes: Excludes two OCR-uncertain figures entirely rather than drafting-then-flagging them: the EPIRB hydrostatic release submersion depth (OCR read '3-10 feet' inside a heavily garbled sentence) and the SART transponder-mode battery duration (the number did not survive OCR at all -- only the 96-hour standby figure is used). Excludes the Morse code page's content beyond SOS, since SOS as a distress symbol is already live (q-cfr-sos-morse-distress) and the rest of that page is a reference chart, not prose. Checked against all 25 live chapter-5-signaling questions before drafting -- basic 'what is an EPIRB/SART/VHF for' definitional questions (q-sig-1 through q-sig-4) were deliberately not re-drafted; this pack focuses on more specific technical facts, procedures, and figures instead.

questions.prompt: In shore lifesaving apparatus operations (rocket line / breeches buoy rescue), what do vertical and horizontal signal motions mean?

choices.text: Vertical motion (or a green star) means "affirmative" (e.g., haul away); horizontal motion (or a red star) means "negative" (e.g., slack away, avast hauling)
Vertical motion means "negative"; horizontal motion means "affirmative"
choices.misconception: Reverses the meaning of the two motions.

choices.text: Both vertical and horizontal motion mean the same thing: stop all operations
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's clear affirmative/negative distinction between the two motions -- they don't share an identical stop-everything meaning.

choices.text: These motions are only used for the landing-place signals, not for shore apparatus operations
choices.misconception: Ignores that vertical/horizontal motion is reused with the same general affirmative/negative logic specifically for shore lifesaving apparatus signals, a related but distinct signal set.

hints: This uses the same vertical-versus-horizontal, green-versus-red logic as the landing-place signals, just applied to a rescue-line operation instead.
questions.explanation: In shore lifesaving apparatus operations, vertical motion of a white flag/light (or a green star signal) means "affirmative" -- specifically that the rocket line is held, the tail block is made fast, the hawser is made fast, a person is in the breeches buoy, or "haul away." Horizontal motion (or a red star signal) means "negative" -- specifically "slack away" or "avast hauling."
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Table of Lifesaving Signals section (manual page ~129); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

promotionHistory.note: Promotion of the 34 questions approved in vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-promotion-review.md after an independent review pass. q-ch5man-shore-apparatus-signals stays in this file, inactive, pending a direct visual check of manual page 129 (flagged as rougher OCR quality). q-ch5man-distress-dye-marker was promoted despite scoring 0.756 similarity against the live q-cfr-orangesmoke-distress -- reviewed by hand and confirmed as a sentence-template artifact (different device, different correct answer), not a real duplicate.
